title: 10 best herbal teas for high cholesterol

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Broad-leaved lime teas helps with high cholesterol because of its unique composition, which is rich in antioxidants, flavonoids, and polyphenols. These compounds have been shown to help reduce the absorption of dietary cholesterol into the bloodstream, while also increasing the excretion of bile acids, which can help lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels. Additionally, herbal broad-leaved lime teas may help improve blood vessel function and increase the body's production of HDL (good) cholesterol, further supporting overall cardiovascular health.
remedy_properties: Hypolipidemic activity: Broad-leaved lime tea has been shown to reduce triglycerides and LDL (bad) cholesterol levels, helping to alleviate hyperlipidemia (high blood lipid levels) associated with cardiovascular disease.

Antioxidant properties: The tea's antioxidant compounds, such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, help protect against oxidative stress and inflammation in the body, which can contribute to high cholesterol levels and cardiovascular damage.

Cardiovascular protection: The tea's bioactive compounds have been found to inhibit platelet aggregation, reduce blood pressure, and improve vasodilation, all of which can help prevent cardiovascular events such as heart attacks and strokes.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in flavonoids and saponins, which help to lower LDL cholesterol levels and improve blood lipid profiles.
Rhyzomes: Contain compounds that inhibit the absorption of dietary cholesterol, reducing its accumulation in the bloodstream.
remedy_recipe: Gather one tablespoon of dried tilia platyphyllos flowers or three teaspoons of fresh flowers.
Heat one cup of water in a saucepan until it reaches its boiling point.
Add the gathered tilia platyphyllos flowers to the heated water and allow them to steep for ten minutes.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup and discard the solids.
Allow the tea to cool down to a comfortable temperature before drinking within five to ten minutes.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Sage teas helps with high cholesterol because it contains compounds that have been shown to lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels and improve overall cardiovascular health. The flavonoids and phenolic acids present in sage tea have been found to inhibit the absorption of dietary cholesterol, reducing its accumulation in the bloodstream. Additionally, sage tea's antioxidants help to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress, which are both contributing factors to high cholesterol. Drinking sage tea as part of a balanced diet may help support healthy lipid profiles and reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease.
remedy_properties: Antioxidants: Sage tea contains antioxidants like rosmarinic acid, which help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the body, both of which can contribute to high cholesterol.
Cholesterol-lowering compounds: Sage tea has been shown to contain compounds that inhibit the production of cholesterol in the liver, helping to lower overall cholesterol levels.

Triglyceride-reducing properties: The polyphenolic compounds present in sage tea have been found to reduce triglyceride levels by inhibiting the activity of enzymes involved in fat synthesis, which can help alleviate high cholesterol symptoms.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in flavonoids and phenolic acids, which help to reduce cholesterol levels by inhibiting the absorption of dietary fats.
Barks: Contain tannins and saponins that have been shown to lower LDL (bad) cholesterol and improve blood lipid profiles.
remedy_recipe: Measure 2 teaspoons of dried sage leaves into a heat-resistant cup or teapot.
Heat one cup of water to a boil and pour it over the sage leaves.
Allow the mixture to steep for 5-7 minutes or until the liquid reaches your desired strength.
Strain the tea into another cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the solids.
Serve the herbal tea hot, sweetened with honey if desired, and consume immediately after preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Licorice teas helps with high cholesterol because it contains flavonoids, which have been shown to help lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels. The tea's active compounds, such as licoricidin and licopyranose, work by inhibiting the production of bile acids in the liver, which leads to a reduction in cholesterol absorption from food. Additionally, herbal licorice teas may also help improve blood lipid profiles by increasing HDL (good) cholesterol levels and decreasing triglycerides. This natural remedy has been traditionally used to support heart health and promote overall well-being.
remedy_properties: Flavonoids: Licorice root contains flavonoids, which have been shown to help lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels by inhibiting the production of cholesterol in the liver.

Glycyrrhizin: Glycyrrhizin, a compound found in licorice root, has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help reduce inflammation in the body, which is often associated with high cholesterol.

Isomaltose: Isomaltose, another compound present in licorice tea, has been shown to inhibit the absorption of dietary cholesterol into the bloodstream, thereby helping to lower overall cholesterol levels.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in glycyrrhizin, a compound that helps reduce triglycerides and LDL (bad) cholesterol levels.
Barks: Containing flavonoids and saponins, which have been shown to improve lipid profiles and lower cholesterol levels.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried glycyrrhiza glabra roots into a clean tea infuser or muslin bag.
Heat one cup of water in a saucepan over medium heat for 5-7 minutes or until it reaches boiling point.
Add the tea infuser or muslin bag to the boiling water and steep for 10-15 minutes to allow the herbs to release their properties.
Remove the tea infuser or muslin bag from the hot liquid and discard the solids; let the tea cool down to a comfortable drinking temperature.
Strain the tea into a cup and drink immediately, ideally three times a day, for at least 8 weeks to help lower high cholesterol levels.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon balm teas helps with high cholesterol because it contains compounds that have been shown to help lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels. The tea's flavonoids, such as kaempferol and quercetin, have been found to improve blood vessel function and reduce inflammation in the body, which can contribute to high cholesterol. Additionally, lemon balm's antioxidant properties may also help protect against oxidative stress, which can damage arteries and increase the risk of cardiovascular disease. By drinking herbal lemon balm teas regularly, individuals may be able to support a healthy cholesterol profile.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Lemon balm tea's antioxidants can help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the body, which may contribute to high cholesterol levels by preventing damage to blood vessels and improving overall cardiovascular health.

Cholesterol-lowering compounds: Lemon balm tea contains compounds like flavonoids and terpenes that have been shown to lower LDL ("bad") cholesterol levels and increase HDL ("good") cholesterol levels, potentially reducing the risk of heart disease.

Cardiovascular relaxation effects: The calming and relaxing properties of lemon balm tea may help reduce stress and anxiety, which can contribute to high blood pressure and cardiovascular disease, ultimately helping to lower cholesterol levels and improve overall cardiovascular health.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the lemon balm plant contain a significant amount of rosmarinic acid, which has been shown to help lower cholesterol levels by inhibiting the absorption of dietary cholesterol.
Leaves: Lemon balm leaves have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the body, which is often associated with high cholesterol.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh melissa officinalis leaves, dried or fresh, to prepare the tea blend.
Combine one teaspoon of dried lemon balm leaves with one cup of boiling water, steeping for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding solids, and add one tablespoon of honey for taste and sweetness.
Drink the tea three times daily, after meals, to help lower cholesterol levels and improve digestion.
Store any leftover tea in an airtight container, refrigerated for up to 3 days or frozen for up to 2 months.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: St John's wort teas helps with high cholesterol because it contains hyperforin, a compound that has been shown to improve blood lipid profiles by increasing levels of HDL (good) cholesterol and decreasing LDL (bad) cholesterol. Additionally, the tea may help reduce inflammation in the body, which is often associated with high cholesterol. The antioxidant properties of St John's wort also help protect against oxidative stress, a factor that can contribute to high cholesterol levels.
remedy_properties: Hyperforin: Helps to lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels by inhibiting the absorption of dietary cholesterol and reducing inflammation in the bloodstream.
Flavonoids: Act as antioxidants, which can help to neutralize free radicals and reduce oxidative stress that can contribute to the development of atherosclerosis (the hardening of arteries).
Iridoid glycosides: May help to improve blood lipid profiles by increasing HDL (good) cholesterol levels and reducing triglyceride levels.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots contain hypericin, a bioactive compound that has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress associated with high cholesterol.

Leaves: The leaves possess flavonoids, such as quercetin and kaempferol, which are known for their ability to improve blood lipid profiles by increasing HDL (good) cholesterol levels and reducing LDL (bad) cholesterol levels.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh st john's wort leaves and flowers in late summer or early fall.
Dry the harvested plant material using a food dehydrator at 90°f for 6 hours.
Measure 2 teaspoons of dried st john's wort flowers and 1 teaspoon of dried leaves per cup of boiling water.
Steep the herbal mixture in boiling water for 5-7 minutes or until desired strength is reached.
Strain the tea into a cup and enjoy warm or chilled within 30 minutes after preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Kansas coneflower teas helps with high cholesterol because of its unique combination of bioactive compounds, including triterpenoid saponins, flavonoids, and phenolic acids. These compounds have been shown to exhibit lipid-lowering properties, reducing both total and LDL ("bad") cholesterol levels by inhibiting cholesterol absorption in the gut and increasing bile acid excretion. Additionally, Kansas coneflower's antioxidants help reduce inflammation in the body, which is often a contributing factor to high cholesterol. Regular consumption of this herbal tea may therefore provide a natural and effective way to support heart health.
remedy_properties: Inflammation reduction: Echinacea's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as cichoric acid and echinacein, can help reduce inflammation in the body, which is associated with increased cholesterol levels.
Cholesterol regulation: The plant's flavonoids and phenolic acids may help regulate cholesterol production and metabolism, potentially lowering LDL ("bad") cholesterol levels.

Antioxidant activity: Echinacea tea's rich antioxidant content can help protect against oxidative stress and damage to blood vessels, which can contribute to high cholesterol and cardiovascular disease.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in saponins, which are natural compounds that can help lower LDL cholesterol levels and reduce inflammation.
Barks: Containing flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been shown to improve blood lipid profiles and reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease.
remedy_recipe: Gather one teaspoon of dried echinacea angustifolia roots and one cup of boiling water.
Steep the dried root mixture in hot water for 10-15 minutes to release its bioactive compounds.
Strain the tea mixture through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a large bowl.
Add one tablespoon of honey as desired for flavor and sweetness and stir well.
Store the cooled tea in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Dandelion teas helps with high cholesterol because it contains taraxasterol, a unique compound that has been shown to lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels by inhibiting its absorption in the gut. Additionally, dandelion root extract has been found to stimulate the liver's natural ability to remove excess cholesterol from the bloodstream, further reducing overall cholesterol levels. This natural approach can be an effective complement to traditional treatment methods for high cholesterol, promoting a healthier heart and cardiovascular system.
remedy_properties: Inulin: Dandelion tea contains inulin, a type of fiber that helps lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels by binding to bile acids and removing them from the body, which in turn reduces cholesterol production in the liver.
Taraxasterol: The tea also contains taraxasterol, a compound that has been shown to inhibit cholesterol absorption in the gut, thereby reducing overall cholesterol levels in the blood.

Ketosterols: Dandelion tea is rich in ketosterols, which are plant compounds that can help lower LDL cholesterol by inhibiting the enzyme responsible for converting dietary cholesterol into bile acids.
remedy_parts: Roots: Dandelion roots contain inulin, a prebiotic fiber that helps lower LDL cholesterol and reduce inflammation in the digestive tract.
Fruits: Dandelion fruits (achenes) have been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce cardiovascular risk factors associated with high cholesterol.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried dandelion roots and leaves according to your preferred tea recipe and dosage requirements.
Clean and chop the harvested dandelion parts into small pieces to release their medicinal properties efficiently.
Combine one teaspoon of chopped dandelion root and two teaspoons of dandelion leaf with one quart of boiling water in a large pot.
Steep the herbal mixture for 10-15 minutes allowing the bioactive compounds to infuse into the tea liquid at room temperature.
Strain the tea through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a separate container and store it in an airtight container refrigerated for up to three days.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Fennel teas helps with high cholesterol because of its unique ability to reduce LDL (bad) cholesterol levels while increasing HDL (good) cholesterol. The tea's flavonoids, particularly quercetin and kaempferol, have been shown to inhibit the absorption of dietary cholesterol in the gut, thereby reducing overall cholesterol levels in the blood. Additionally, fennel's fiber content helps bind to bile acids, which can further reduce LDL cholesterol production. By drinking fennel tea regularly, individuals with high cholesterol may experience a noticeable decrease in their lipid profiles over time.
remedy_properties: Fiber content: Fennel tea contains soluble fiber, which helps to bind to bile acids in the digestive tract and remove them from the body, thereby reducing cholesterol levels.

Antioxidant compounds: Fennel tea is rich in antioxidant compounds such as quercetin and kaempferol, which help to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the blood vessels, preventing the buildup of plaque that can contribute to high cholesterol.

Cardiac glycosides: Fennel tea contains cardiac glycosides, such as anethole, which have been shown to lower triglyceride levels and improve lipid profiles by inhibiting the activity of enzymes involved in cholesterol production.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in fiber and flavonoids, fennel roots help lower LDL cholesterol levels and improve overall cardiovascular health.
Seeds: Fennel seeds contain essential oils with anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce triglycerides and LDL cholesterol levels.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried foeniculum vulgare leaves and flowers from a trusted source.
Combine one teaspoon of crushed fennel herb with one cup of boiling water in a tea infuser.
Allow the mixture to steep for 5-7 minutes to release the bioactive compounds and flavonoids.
Strain the tea into a cup and discard the solids while preserving the clear liquid extract.
Consume the herbal fennel tea within 24 hours of preparation or store it in an airtight container.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Wormwood teas helps with high cholesterol because it contains sesquiterpenes, which have been shown to lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels by inhibiting the production of cholesterol in the liver. Additionally, wormwood has been found to increase HDL (good) cholesterol, improving overall lipid profiles and reducing the risk of cardiovascular disease. Its antioxidant properties also help protect against inflammation and oxidative stress, further contributing to its cholesterol-lowering benefits. Regular consumption of wormwood tea may be a natural and effective way to support heart health.
remedy_properties: Therapeutic compounds like sesquiterpene lactones: These compounds have been shown to reduce LDL (bad) cholesterol levels by inhibiting the absorption of dietary cholesterol and promoting its excretion from the body.

Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects: Wormwood tea's anti-inflammatory properties may help reduce inflammation in the blood vessels, which can contribute to high cholesterol, while its antioxidants may help protect against oxidative stress and damage caused by free radicals.

Hepatoprotective activity: Wormwood has been traditionally used to support liver health, and its hepatoprotective effects may help improve liver function and reduce the production of cholesterol in the liver, ultimately leading to lower overall cholesterol levels.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of wormwood contain sesquiterpenes, which have been shown to help lower triglycerides and reduce inflammation in the body.
Barks: Wormwood barks have been traditionally used to treat digestive issues, including high cholesterol, due to their ability to stimulate bile production and improve fat metabolism.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried wormwood leaves and stems from a trusted supplier or garden in quantities of 2-3 tablespoons.
Combine the harvested wormwood with boiling water in a large pot, steeping for 10-15 minutes to release its medicinal properties.
Strain the tea mixture using a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a separate container, discarding solids and reserving liquid.
Add one teaspoon of honey to sweeten the tea and mix well, allowing it to dissolve before serving.
Enjoy 2-3 cups of the herbal wormwood tea per day, sipping hot or cold, for a minimum of 6 weeks to achieve desired cholesterol-lowering benefits.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Blueberry teas helps with high cholesterol because of its potent antioxidants and flavonoids. These compounds help to increase levels of HDL (good) cholesterol, while reducing LDL (bad) cholesterol. Additionally, blueberries contain anthocyanins, which have been shown to improve blood flow and lower blood pressure, further contributing to reduced risk of heart disease. Drinking blueberry tea regularly may also help to reduce inflammation in the body, a known contributor to high cholesterol.
remedy_properties: Antioxidants: Blueberry tea is rich in antioxidants like anthocyanins, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which help neutralize free radicals that contribute to inflammation and oxidative stress, reducing the risk of cardiovascular disease associated with high cholesterol.

Flavonoids: The flavonoid compounds present in blueberry tea, such as quercetin and kaempferol, have been shown to inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes and improve lipid profiles by lowering LDL (bad) cholesterol levels and increasing HDL (good) cholesterol.

Phenolic acids: Blueberry tea contains phenolic acids like chlorogenic acid, which has been found to reduce triglycerides and LDL cholesterol levels, while also improving insulin sensitivity and glucose metabolism, thus helping to regulate blood lipid profiles.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots contain bioactive compounds such as anthocyanins and flavonoids, which help to reduce inflammation and improve blood lipid profiles.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes have been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various cardiovascular diseases, including high cholesterol, due to their ability to lower triglycerides and LDL levels.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried vaccinium myrtillus leaves in the morning after dew has evaporated for optimal potency.
Rinse one teaspoon of dried blueberry leaves or two teaspoons of fresh leaves with cold filtered water to remove impurities.
Steep the rinsed blueberry leaves in a teapot or tea infuser with 1 cup boiling water for 5-7 minutes to extract active compounds.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a large bowl or individual cups and discard solids immediately after use.
Store cooled herbal tea in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to three days or freeze for up to six months for future consumption.

intro_desc: Herbal teas for high cholesterol are a natural and effective way to support heart health by lowering LDL (bad) cholesterol levels and improving overall circulation. These teas work by inhibiting the absorption of cholesterol in the gut, increasing the excretion of bile salts, and improving blood lipid profiles. Examples of herbal teas that help with high cholesterol include turmeric tea, which has anti-inflammatory properties, and dandelion root tea, which helps to lower triglycerides. Drinking these teas regularly can lead to improved cardiovascular health, reduced risk of heart disease, and a better overall quality of life.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat high cholesterol with herbal teas is by incorporating them into your daily routine. Hibiscus tea has been shown to lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels by reducing triglycerides and increasing HDL (good) cholesterol. Ginger tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the blood vessels, which can contribute to high cholesterol. Turmeric tea contains curcumin, a powerful antioxidant that helps improve lipid profiles. Drink 2-3 cups of these teas per day for optimal results.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to high cholesterol that are treated with herbal teas are those related to lipid metabolism, blood circulation, and cardiovascular health. Certain herbal teas, such as hibiscus tea, have been shown to help lower LDL (bad) cholesterol levels and triglycerides. Other herbs like turmeric, ginkgo biloba, and green tea may also help improve blood flow and reduce the risk of heart disease. These teas can be consumed as a complementary therapy to support overall cardiovascular health.
